defaultPregnancy: Pregnancy and breastfeeding effects vary by medication. Talk with your obstetrician or primary care clinician before starting or continuing any prescription or OTC drug.
defaultSideEffects: All medications can cause side effects. Read your pharmacy label and call your clinician if you notice anything unusual.
defaultSubstance: This substance may change how your body handles many medications. Use caution and ask a pharmacist or doctor before combining.

metformin.pregnancy: Often used in pregnancy when clinically indicated, but insulin is preferred in some cases. Your clinician should guide dosing.

substances.alcohol: Heavy alcohol use with metformin raises the risk of lactic acidosis.
substances.cannabis: Limited formal data; monitor blood sugar if you use cannabis regularly.
substances.tobacco: Smoking can worsen diabetes control; not a direct interaction but affects overall risk.

lisinopril.pregnancy: ACE inhibitors can harm a developing baby. Do not use during pregnancy unless a specialist directs otherwise.

substances.alcohol: Alcohol can intensify blood-pressure lowering and dizziness.
substances.cannabis: May add to dizziness or lightheadedness when standing.
substances.tobacco: Smoking worsens cardiovascular risk; quit support is recommended.

warfarin.pregnancy: Generally avoided in pregnancy because of fetal bleeding risk. Requires specialist management if absolutely needed.

substances.alcohol: Alcohol destabilizes INR and increases bleeding risk.
substances.cannabis: May affect bleeding risk; inform your anticoagulation clinic.
substances.tobacco: Smoking changes warfarin metabolism; dose adjustments may be needed when quitting or starting.

escitalopram.pregnancy: Discuss risks and benefits with your psychiatrist or OB; some SSRIs are used in pregnancy with monitoring.

substances.alcohol: Alcohol worsens sedation and mood instability.
substances.cannabis: Combined CNS effects; may worsen anxiety or sedation in some people.
substances.tobacco: Smoking can lower escitalopram levels slightly; tell your prescriber if you quit.

ibuprofen.pregnancy: Avoid in the third trimester and use the lowest effective dose earlier in pregnancy only if your clinician approves.

substances.alcohol: Increases stomach bleeding and ulcer risk when combined.
substances.cannabis: Both can cause dizziness; use caution with driving.
substances.tobacco: Smoking adds to stomach and cardiovascular risk when using NSAIDs regularly.

sideEffects: hair changes when dose is adjusting
levothyroxine.pregnancy: Thyroid hormone needs often increase in pregnancy; requires lab monitoring and dose adjustment.

substances.alcohol: No major direct interaction; take levothyroxine on an empty stomach as directed.
substances.cannabis: No well-established interaction; maintain consistent thyroid lab follow-up.
substances.tobacco: Smoking can affect thyroid hormone levels; inform your endocrinologist.

montelukast.pregnancy: Sometimes used for asthma in pregnancy when benefits outweigh risks; confirm with your clinician.

substances.alcohol: No major direct interaction; avoid alcohol if it triggers asthma.
substances.cannabis: Smoked cannabis may worsen respiratory symptoms in asthma.
substances.tobacco: Tobacco smoke worsens asthma; quitting improves medication effectiveness.

atorvastatin.pregnancy: Statins are not recommended during pregnancy or while trying to conceive.

substances.alcohol: Heavy alcohol use increases liver strain with statins.
substances.cannabis: Limited data; report muscle pain to your clinician.
substances.tobacco: Smoking adds cardiovascular risk statins are meant to reduce.

omeprazole.pregnancy: Often considered when needed in pregnancy; use lowest effective dose and duration.

substances.alcohol: Alcohol can irritate the stomach; PPIs do not remove alcohol-related ulcer risk.
substances.cannabis: May affect stomach emptying in some users; clinical significance is unclear.
substances.tobacco: Smoking worsens reflux and ulcer disease.

albuterol.pregnancy: Short-acting bronchodilators are commonly used in pregnancy for asthma relief when needed.

substances.alcohol: Alcohol is not a direct contraindication but can worsen dehydration.
substances.cannabis: Smoked cannabis may trigger bronchospasm in sensitive individuals.
substances.tobacco: Smoking reduces lung function and makes rescue inhalers less effective over time.
